title: General Rules & Responsibilities
content: All drivers must hold a valid Zimbabwean driving licence or recognised international permit.
Vehicles must be roadworthy and have a valid certificate of fitness.
Third-party insurance is compulsory for all motor vehicles.
Drivers must carry their licence, vehicle registration, and insurance documents at all times.
Seat belts must be worn by the driver and all passengers at all times.
Using a hand-held mobile phone while driving is prohibited.
Children under 3 years must be secured in an approved child seat.
It is an offence to drive under the influence of alcohol or drugs.
Drivers must obey all traffic signs, signals, and road markings.
Report all accidents to the nearest police station within 24 hours.

title: Speed Limits
content: Built-up / Urban areas: 60 km/h unless otherwise posted.
Rural / Open roads: 80 km/h unless otherwise posted.
Highways / Dual carriageways: 120 km/h unless otherwise posted.
School zones: 40 km/h during school hours when signs are displayed.
Construction / Road-work zones: Speed as indicated by temporary signs, typically 40-60 km/h.
Towing another vehicle: Maximum 30 km/h.
Heavy commercial vehicles (above 3 500 kg): 80 km/h maximum on all roads.
Minibuses and light commercial vehicles: 100 km/h maximum on highways.
Speeding fines are issued on the spot and increase with severity of the offence.
Speed cameras are in operation on major highways and urban arterials.

title: Overtaking & Passing
content: Overtake on the right only (Zimbabwe drives on the left).
Do not overtake when approaching a hill crest, curve, or intersection.
Do not overtake where the road is marked with a solid continuous centre line on your side.
Ensure the road ahead is clear for a sufficient distance before overtaking.
Signal your intention to overtake and check mirrors and blind spots.
Return to the left lane only when the overtaken vehicle is visible in your rear-view mirror.
Do not exceed the speed limit while overtaking.
Give way to vehicles already overtaking you; do not accelerate to block them.
Never overtake at or near pedestrian crossings, bridges, or level crossings.
Flash headlights or sound your horn to warn the vehicle ahead when overtaking on rural roads.

title: Traffic Signals & Lights
content: RED: Stop behind the stop line and wait. Do not proceed until green.
RED + AMBER: Prepare to go but do not proceed until green appears.
GREEN: Proceed if the way is clear. Give way to pedestrians and vehicles still in the intersection.
AMBER (alone): Stop unless you are so close that stopping would be dangerous.
GREEN ARROW: You may proceed in the direction of the arrow, giving way to pedestrians.
FLASHING RED: Treat as a stop sign; stop completely and proceed when safe.
FLASHING AMBER: Proceed with caution; the intersection is uncontrolled.
Traffic lights out of order: Treat the intersection as a four-way stop.
At intersections with both lights and signs, obey the traffic lights.
Pedestrian signals with countdown timers must be obeyed; do not start crossing on a flashing red hand.

title: Pedestrian Crossings
content: Always give way to pedestrians on a marked crossing.
Slow down and be prepared to stop when approaching a pedestrian crossing.
Do not overtake another vehicle that has stopped at a pedestrian crossing.
At traffic lights, give way to pedestrians crossing when you turn.
Flashing amber lights near crossings mean slow down and give way to pedestrians.
Do not park or stop within 10 metres of a pedestrian crossing.
Sound your horn only to warn pedestrians of danger, not to rush them.
At school crossings, obey scholar patrol signs and crossing guards.
Zebra crossings: Pedestrians have right of way once they step onto the crossing.
Be especially vigilant in areas near hospitals, shopping centres, and bus stops.

title: Lane Discipline
content: Drive in the left lane unless overtaking or turning right.
On a multi-lane road, use the left lane for normal driving and the right lane for overtaking.
Do not weave between lanes or change lanes without signalling.
Use slip roads and acceleration lanes to merge safely onto highways.
Do not straddle lane markings; stay entirely within your lane.
When approaching a roundabout, choose your lane based on your exit.
Bus lanes: Do not drive in marked bus lanes during restricted hours.
On three-lane roads, the middle lane is for overtaking only; return to the left lane promptly.
Use turning lanes where provided; do not cut across lanes at intersections.
In heavy traffic, maintain your lane position and proceed in turn.

title: Driving in Adverse Conditions
content: Rain: Reduce speed, increase following distance, and use dipped headlights.
Fog: Use fog lights (if fitted) or dipped headlights. Drive slowly and stay alert.
Night driving: Use headlights between sunset and sunrise. Dip lights for oncoming traffic.
Flooded roads: Do not attempt to cross if the water depth is unknown. If you must cross, drive slowly in low gear.
Gravel roads: Reduce speed, keep a safe following distance to avoid stone damage.
Dust: Slow down when visibility is reduced and use dipped headlights.
Strong winds: Hold the steering wheel firmly; be cautious around large vehicles.
Sun glare: Use sun visors and keep your windscreen clean to reduce glare.
If conditions are dangerously poor, pull off the road safely and wait until they improve.
Always check weather forecasts before long journeys.

title: Alcohol & Drug Laws
content: The legal blood alcohol limit in Zimbabwe is 0.08 g per 100 ml of blood (80 mg%).
Driving under the influence is a criminal offence carrying heavy penalties.
Penalties include fines, licence suspension, imprisonment, or a combination.
Police may conduct random roadside sobriety checks and breathalyser tests.
Refusing a breathalyser test is treated the same as a positive result.
Prescription and over-the-counter drugs that impair driving must not be taken before driving.
It is an offence to be in charge of a vehicle while intoxicated, even if not driving.
Repeat offenders face increased penalties and longer licence suspensions.
If you plan to drink, arrange alternative transport; designate a sober driver.
Alcohol-related accidents that cause injury or death carry sentences of up to 10 years.

title: Licensing Requirements
content: Minimum age for a learner's licence (Class 4 - light motor vehicle): 16 years.
Minimum age for a full driving licence (Class 4): 18 years.
A learner's permit is valid for 12 months from the date of issue.
Learner drivers must be accompanied by a licenced driver at all times.
Learner drivers must display "L" plates on the front and rear of the vehicle.
The driving test includes a road signs test (oral), yard test, and road test.
A provisional licence is issued after passing the driving test, valid for 2 years.
Licences are renewable at the Central Vehicle Registry (CVR) or designated offices.
Foreign licences are valid for 90 days; thereafter you must obtain a Zimbabwean licence.
Class 1 (motorcycle), Class 2 (heavy vehicle), and Class 3 (bus) licences have additional requirements.
